One of the most challenging and impressive projects I’ve worked on was scaling Curaa.in from a traditional doctor recruitment agency into a data-driven, AI-assisted platform serving 40,000+ healthcare professionals.

Problem:
India’s healthcare hiring was fragmented—slow TATs, manual processes, and poor candidate-employer alignment.

Technical & Non-Technical Hurdles:

No tech team; had to bootstrap everything.

Fragmented doctor data; had to build and clean a verified database from scratch.

Employer hesitation towards digital hiring workflows.

How I Solved It:

Built a verified talent database of 41,500+ professionals, 95% doctors.

Introduced AI-led tools (like resume parsing, matching, and ATS readiness scoring).

Shifted positioning from "agency" to "healthcare hiring partner" to build trust.

Outcome:
Recruited 1,250+ doctors for top brands like PharmEasy, Mosaic Wellness, and Asian Heart. Now laying foundations to scale this into an AI-first B2B SaaS platform.

I'm Raul John Aju – an AI innovator, global consultant, and tech educator from Kochi, Kerala. My journey into AI started young, but it took off when I created viral educational content about AI tools and automation. From there, I was invited to take sessions across schools and companies, which eventually led me to build my first legal AI assistant – NyayaSathi, aimed at helping Indian citizens understand and access the law.

Soon after, I expanded this to the UAE with JustEase, a 24/7 AI-powered legal assistant available via WhatsApp and web, built with complete data privacy – no APIs, no external servers. This work caught the attention of ministers in both India and Dubai, and I’ve had the honour of presenting my solutions to the Kerala CM, Law Minister, and the AI Minister of Dubai.

I’m the founder of AIRealm Technologies Pvt Ltd, through which I’ve built several AI products like:

Mebot – my AI clone that can think and speak like me

Investo – an AI investment advisor

ZapGap – an AI-powered cloud cost optimizer

FeedFye & StoryScince – automation tools for Instagram and LinkedIn content


I’ve won the Buildathon by 100X Engineers, India’s biggest AI hackathon, and was Runner-up globally at ERPA. I’m also one of the 3 national mentors for Bharat AI, conducting workshops across India and teaching thousands of students and professionals how to go from AI-curious to AI-serious.

To date, I’ve taught AI to 1000+ institutions globally, spoken at major events like TEDx, Google, and Huddle Global, and collaborated with 400+ companies. Some of my Instagram and YouTube videos have crossed millions of views, with one reel alone doing 2M+.

My mission is simple: Make AI practical, responsible, and available to everyone – not just big companies, but students, small businesses, and governments. I build, teach, and inspire through code, content, and conversation.

By the end of the cohort, I want to walk away with a tighter grip on AI product engineering, better structure in how I build, and a deeper understanding of what it takes to go from 0 to 1 and beyond – not just as a creator, but as a founder and leader in AI.

column47:
One of the most challenging and rewarding projects I’ve worked on is JustEase — an AI-powered legal assistant designed specifically for the UAE. The goal was to build a system that could understand everyday legal questions (from tourists, residents, or business owners) and respond in real-time with accurate UAE Penal Code articles — all while maintaining zero data leakage and 24/7 uptime.

The Problem:

In the UAE, legal help is expensive, language barriers are common, and many people don’t even know which law applies to their issue. I wanted to solve that using AI — but unlike other AI tools, I couldn’t rely on cloud APIs like OpenAI due to privacy laws and local compliance.

Technical Challenges:

Building a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system that runs completely offline, using only local UAE law PDFs.

Fine-tuning query rewriting so that vague WhatsApp-style questions like "Can I get arrested for a fight in Dubai?" are converted into proper legal queries like "Which UAE Penal Code articles apply to physical assault?"

Optimizing it to run on limited resources using Ollama with LLaMA 3:8B and Nomadic embeddings, all on local servers.


Non-Technical Hurdles:

Trust: Getting people (and even officials) to believe AI could handle sensitive legal issues responsibly.

User experience: Designing a flow that works on platforms like WhatsApp or Instagram — simple, conversational, but still accurate and legally sound.

Compliance: Making sure the system never generates hallucinated or biased answers, since it's used in real legal contexts.


Outcome:

JustEase now runs fully locally, offering 24/7 legal help with three modes — Casual, Emergency, and Law Reference — each responding differently based on the user's urgency and context. It’s used by tourists, residents, and even small businesses to get instant legal clarity. The platform got attention from UAE ministers, Indian legal experts, and is being considered for government-level deployment.

For me, this wasn’t just about building tech — it was about making the law more accessible, private, and human through AI. That’s what made it the most challenging and meaningful project I’ve done so far.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I’ve worked on is building a fully automated AI-powered YouTube channel in the luxury niche. The goal was to create a scalable content engine that could consistently generate high-quality, monetizable videos—without showing my face or hiring a team.

The Problem:
I needed to combine storytelling, market research, scripting, voiceover, video editing, and thumbnail design—entirely through automation or AI tools. The real challenge was achieving quality that matches or beats human-created content, while managing every step solo.

Technical Hurdles:
Automation Stack Complexity: I had to evaluate and integrate multiple AI tools like ChatGPT for scripting, ElevenLabs for voiceovers, and Runway + After Effects for video editing. Syncing these tools to maintain a consistent tone, pacing, and storytelling style was not straightforward.

Rendering Quality: Ensuring cinematic quality using AI-generated visuals and kinetic text required learning advanced After Effects workflows, scripting animations, and understanding export formats to preserve quality.

Voice + Visual Sync: Matching AI voiceovers with dynamic visuals while keeping engagement high took trial and error, especially with timing text animations to speech.

Non-Technical Hurdles:
Creativity Burnout: Doing everything solo—content planning, research, writing, design—was mentally taxing. To solve this, I created reusable templates and a systemized production pipeline.

Imposter Syndrome: I doubted whether AI-based content could actually succeed in such a high-quality niche. But continuous testing, improving video retention, and small early wins kept me going.

Learning Curve: I had to self-learn a lot—like storytelling frameworks, pacing psychology, monetization policies, and YouTube SEO—all while managing my other commitments.

The Outcome:
I eventually built a full production workflow where I could go from idea to high-end video within hours—automated, brand-consistent, and ready for scaling. I also created variations of this system for other niches and applied these skills to other business ideas like Kuponx AI, a smart coupon search engine, and gamified math quiz apps.

This project taught me how to merge creativity, automation, and business thinking—and gave me the confidence to solve complex, multi-disciplinary problems in the real world.

I’m currently exploring two focused applications of Generative AI:

1. Personal Branding Automation Wrapper:
My goal is to build a streamlined, end-to-end system that allows founders and creators to automate their entire content creation and distribution workflow. The system would work like this:

A script is generated, fine-tuned on the user’s personal data or voice.

A talking-head video is created using AI avatars and custom-trained voice clones (e.g., ElevenLabs).

The footage is then edited using an automated editing layer.

It’s published across social platforms using AI-driven scheduling tools, paired with automated engagement (comments, replies, DMs via tools like ManyChat or custom agents).

A user-friendly dashboard would allow full control and customization.
This would make personal branding scalable, efficient, and less time-consuming—especially for non-creators like tech founders.

2. Automated Lead Generation & Sales System for Agencies:
I also aim to build a fully automated lead generation and conversion system for my own agency, which can later be offered as a service to other small businesses. This would involve:

An AI-powered lead generator and qualifier.

Automated workflows for filtering and scoring leads.

A sales agent (AI or human-assisted) that takes over for demos or consultations.

Post-call follow-ups, onboarding, and retention workflows—all streamlined through automation.
This would reduce the manual burden on founders while improving conversion consistency and scale.

These are just starting points, and I’m aware others are building in similar spaces. But I want to innovate by making the process more seamless, founder-friendly, and results-driven—backed by my firsthand experience in video production, agency operations, and branding.

column47:
One of the most challenging and rewarding projects I recently worked on was an AI-generated film for the Uttar Pradesh government. While I can’t share specific details due to confidentiality, the project involved bringing rough conceptual sketches to life using a generative AI video pipeline.

The creative process involved multiple stages: we began by creating hand-drawn illustrations, then used AI-based image generation to refine and enhance the visual narrative, and finally translated those into animated sequences using video generation tools.

The biggest technical hurdle we faced was OCR (Optical Character Recognition). At the time, ChatGPT and other tools were not fully equipped to understand OCR-based inputs with high accuracy—especially when dealing with context-specific visual elements embedded in regional or stylized fonts. I explored multiple tools for this, including experimenting with OCR pipelines, and even considered trying out options like Compfy UI. We also ran into limitations using Flux, which made it harder to achieve precision in our early iterations.

Another major challenge was video quality. We started with Kling 1.6, which showed promise but produced noticeable artifacts—especially problematic given the complexity of some shots, like large-scale war scenes and mythological environments. We attempted alternatives like Runway and closely monitored platforms like Hugging Face to identify emerging open-source models that could help. Luckily, during production, the release of Kling 2.0 brought massive improvements in quality. That update enabled us to rework several complex sequences and elevate the film's overall fidelity.

Ultimately, the project came together through a combination of adaptability, constant community tracking, and persistent testing across evolving AI tools. It was an intense learning experience that showed me how rapidly the generative AI space is evolving—and how important it is to stay flexible and deeply curious.

One of the most challenging and impressive projects I’ve worked on was designing an emotion-driven virtual reality experience to study how people respond to different environments. The goal was to collect emotional data from users as they moved through various virtual spaces, so those insights could be used to design better real-world cities.

The problem: We needed to create an experience that felt natural and immersive while also collecting accurate emotional feedback. It had to work across different user types some experienced in VR, some not and ensure the emotions we recorded were real, not reactions to discomfort or confusion.

Technical challenges:

Designing a smooth and realistic VR path that didn’t cause motion sickness.

Making interfaces in VR that were easy to use without breaking the immersion.

Creating dashboards for different teams (researchers, planners, data providers) that visualized emotional responses clearly.

Non-technical challenges:

Mapping locations that would trigger a wide range of emotional reactions.

Defining participant profiles that reflected a balanced mix of age, gender, and experience.

Collaborating across teams who had very different goals, some focused on city planning, others on tech or research.

One of the most challenging and impressive projects I worked on was during my business consulting role at Teralco Group.

The problem involved analyzing user data from over 150 data sources across different markets to redesign an existing product for better performance. The product was not generating the expected user engagement, and the leadership needed clear, data-driven insights to make product changes and drive growth.

Technical hurdles:
The data was scattered across multiple platforms with varying formats, structures, and data quality. We had to clean, normalize, and unify the data before any meaningful analysis could begin. Working with large-scale datasets across the USA and EU markets also brought in complexities related to compliance and privacy. I used data analysis and visualization tools to segment users, analyze behavioral patterns, and identify key bottlenecks affecting engagement.

Non-technical hurdles:
The leadership team had multiple stakeholders, each with different priorities. The sales, product, and marketing teams had their own viewpoints on what was driving the problem. I had to present the insights in a way that balanced all these perspectives, while building alignment around data-backed recommendations. Communication and stakeholder management were key here to get buy-in for the proposed changes.

How I succeeded:
We delivered a complete redesign of the product’s user experience and targeting approach, backed by clear data narratives. Within three months, the changes led to over 30% increase in user engagement across both regions, and more than 1000 new monthly trial sign-ups. This project taught me how to handle both the technical complexities of large data-driven projects and the equally challenging task of building stakeholder alignment in cross-functional environments.

[24]7.ai is a tech-enabled CX solutions provider servicing some of the largest fortune 500 companies in the world by providing CX solutions to them.  My role as a social media manager was to drive metric-driven distribution for the brand.

Social media at our organisation was traditionally viewed as a branding tool. Good for reach and visibility, but not necessarily tied to business outcomes. 

My goal was to shift that mindset and prove that social could actively drive qualified talent acquisition across roles while also strengthening the employer brand. The challenge was clear: how do we make social media both strategic and authentic, appealing to potential candidates, current employees, and industry stakeholders alike?

To solve this, we built a full-funnel strategy from the ground up. We aligned content themes with hiring goals, created narrative-led posts showcasing culture, impact, and employee voices, and introduced formats like reels, carousels, and leadership storytelling to bring the brand to life. On the performance side, we optimised paid campaigns for reach, relevance, and cost-efficiency, bringing down cost per lead by over 50%. We also launched the Founder’s Forum, an internal LinkedIn advocacy program that activated over 80% of our leadership team, amplifying our credibility and organic reach.

The results spoke for themselves. Instagram grew from 6K to 25 K+ followers, social content delivered over 57 million impressions, and our engagement rate climbed to 29%. Most importantly, we tripled the number of joiners sourced via social media, proving that employer branding, when done right, drives real business value, not just likes and follows.

We are now on the path to automate our recruitment workflows & drive even more conversion from social media as a source for our mass-hiring.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I’ve worked on was building my plus-size fashion brand from scratch—at a time when size inclusivity wasn’t even part of mainstream conversation in India. The idea was bold but came with immense challenges.

The problem: There was a massive gap in the market, but the customer base was underserved, skeptical, and lacked confidence in online shopping for fashion. Manufacturing was a nightmare—factories weren’t used to larger size blocks, and creating flattering fits required deep technical understanding of patterns and body proportions.

Technical hurdles:

Creating sizing systems beyond the standard XS-XL range.

Managing inventory for a wide size range while maintaining profitability.

Educating tailors and production units unfamiliar with plus-size specs.

Non-technical hurdles:

Changing perceptions of beauty and encouraging body confidence.

Building trust with a sensitive customer segment through storytelling and representation.

Running operations as a bootstrapped founder without a background in fashion tech or VC funding.

Despite these challenges, we grew organically, gained loyal customers, and built a brand that made women feel seen and celebrated. Though I eventually exited due to sustainability issues, the journey taught me everything about grit, empathy, and creating businesses rooted in emotional value.

I’m Akash Jadhav, founder and CEO of Indian Farmer — a digital-first agri-education platform I co-founded with my close friend Santosh Jadhav.

We started this journey 7 years ago with one simple belief: farming in India needs to be respected, profitable, and future-ready. What began as experiments in content and education has now grown into a system-led movement — focused on clean food, farmer clarity, and practical knowledge.

Today, our content reaches over 10–12 million farmers across platforms. We’ve launched our first paid course and are building a full roadmap of practical, ROI-driven agri-courses. We’ve also worked with top agriculture brands for collaborations and campaigns that actually add value to our farmer community.

But this is not just about content or business. Indian Farmer is a grassroots movement — to restore dignity in farming, and change how the world sees farmers… and how farmers see themselves.

Now, I’m here to learn how AI can help us take this to the next level — to scale our content, automate our systems, and improve farmer outcomes with real intelligence and clarity.

Most challenging project: Building Indian Farmer

A digital-first agri-education platform to educate and empower rural farmers through practical, trustworthy content.

Core Problem
There was no platform offering real, ground-level education to farmers.
Most players repeated outdated advice or approached it from a top-down, non-practical lens.
We wanted to change that — with trust, tech, and relatable storytelling.

Technical Hurdles
	•	Started with zero experience in video, editing, LMS, or online education
	•	Learned content creation, digital marketing, and systems from scratch
	•	Bootstrapped everything — without any external support, office, or mentors

Non-Technical Hurdles
	•	Convincing rural farmers to trust and pay for online knowledge
	•	Hiring and building a lean but committed team
	•	Navigating emotional ups and downs — because this work is personal
	•	Staying consistent without shortcuts or big budgets

How We Moved Forward
	•	Reached over 10 million farmers via YouTube, Instagram, and Facebook
	•	Launched our first paid course (Tomato Farming) with 600+ organic users
	•	Partnered with major agri-brands for paid content and collaborations
	•	Used AI and automation to stay lean and consistent
	•	Built every system in-house with a small team and minimal resources

What Made It Hard
Running a real business in an ignored niche
Learning how to hire, plan, and execute — while being the creator, leader, and learner
Doing it all from a village, without shortcuts

We’re still building. Still trying. But we’re clear, rooted, and moving.

column45:
Throughout my career, I’ve explored a wide range of areas spanning genomic and clinical bioinformatics, where I developed automated pipelines for high-throughput sequencing, variant interpretation, and precision medicine applications. I’ve led research projects on complex diseases such as epilepsy, colorectal cancer, Crohn’s disease, obesity, familial hypercholesterolemia, and celiac disease, utilizing whole exome sequencing, gene expression profiling, and systems biology approaches. My work has also included structural bioinformatics, where I applied homology modeling, protein docking, and molecular dynamics simulations, particularly in drug discovery and COVID-19-related projects. In parallel, I’ve built web-based tools and automated workflows that integrate AI into bioinformatics, streamlining routine tasks and data interpretation.

Currently, I’m exploring how deep learning can enhance clinical genomics and improve decision-making in genetic testing labs. I’m also working on integrating AI and machine learning into multi-omics data analysis, particularly for rare disease studies like epilepsy, where we’ve built large variant databases and predictive models. A key focus now is developing AI-powered systems that automate bioinformatics processes end-to-end from data retrieval to pipeline execution pushing the boundaries of what’s possible in clinical research and digital health
column47:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I’ve worked on was building a machine learning model for genetic variant classification—an area that required me to step far outside my academic background. My Ph.D. is in biochemistry with a specialization in bioinformatics, but I didn’t come from a core computational or programming background. In the early days, before the rise of AI tools, I learned to code by adapting scripts from other projects—often without formal guidance—because bioinformatics tutorials and resources were scarce.

This particular project involved scraping data from multiple genomic databases and research platforms, followed by extensive curation and selection of meaningful data points to train the model. The process was technically intense and manually exhausting. I had to overcome several hurdles: inconsistent data formats, lack of structured APIs, missing values, and the challenge of mapping biological relevance to machine-readable features. Non-technical hurdles included a steep learning curve and the mental fatigue of trial-and-error coding.

What changed everything for me was the arrival of AI tools. They allowed me to automate large parts of the workflow—from data cleaning to prompt-based script generation—dramatically reducing development time and improving output quality. Today, many of my routine bioinformatics analyses are fully automated with minimal code writing, and this transformation has empowered me to take on even more complex problems with confidence and speed.

This journey not only tested my resilience and problem-solving but also demonstrated how curiosity and adaptability can turn limitations into strengths.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I’ve worked on was “Meri TB Ki Kahani”—a first-of-its-kind national storytelling campaign aimed at destigmatizing tuberculosis in India by amplifying the voices of TB survivors through mobile-first video storytelling.

The Problem:
Tuberculosis remains one of India's deadliest and most misunderstood diseases. While government data and health reports provided numbers, survivors' stories were largely missing from the public narrative. The stigma around TB meant that many patients stayed silent, leading to late diagnosis, social isolation, and loss of livelihood.

 Technical & Non-Technical Challenges: Technical Hurdles:
Although the platform itself was a simple web-based video submission portal, the automation, content moderation, and backend development required to scale it securely was complex.

We needed to build in multi-language support, mobile responsiveness, and video upload capabilities for low-bandwidth users across rural India.

The cost and time of building and customizing this platform—with limited resources and tight deadlines—became a significant constraint.

Integrating backend systems for review, tagging, approvals, and campaign analytics demanded deep coordination between developers and content teams.
Stigma and Silence: Convincing survivors to share their personal experiences on camera was emotionally and socially sensitive.

Access & Geography: Reaching grassroots survivors in remote areas, many without reliable internet, language fluency, or digital literacy.

Training Survivors: We needed to turn everyday people into mobile storytellers—teaching them how to shoot, frame, and voice their stories using just their phones.

Coordination Across Stakeholders: The project required close collaboration with the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, WHO India, and grassroots NGOs, while ensuring the voices stayed authentic and not over-produced.

Campaign Design: We had to create a scalable format that was emotionally compelling, easily shareable, and built trust online—without professional crews.

✅ How We Succeeded:
We trained over 50+ TB survivors across India to tell their own stories using their phones.

Developed a safe, consent-based storytelling framework so that participants had full control of their narrative.

Partnered with Meta and YouTube to amplify the stories—resulting in millions of views and engagements.

The campaign became a case study for health storytelling and was featured in multiple policy roundtables.

Most importantly, several storytellers became TB ambassadors, encouraging others to seek treatment and break the silence.

Impact:
“Meri TB Ki Kahani” didn’t just tell stories—it shifted narratives, empowered survivors, and contributed to policy conversations about patient-centric healthcare communication in India.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I worked on was the generation and analysis of high-resolution multispectral imagery and CartoSAT stereo data for a 3,300 sq. km area around Mathura district.

The goal was to extract actionable spatial insights using 5m multispectral and 2.5m stereo imagery, which required precise data preprocessing, georeferencing, and elevation modeling. The technical hurdles included handling large data volumes, ensuring geometric accuracy across datasets, and automating image classification for land use patterns. We used remote sensing techniques and custom scripts to process and analyze the data efficiently.

On the non-technical side, coordinating with multiple stakeholders, managing tight timelines, and aligning outputs with government objectives added complexity. Effective communication and iterative feedback loops were key.

We successfully delivered detailed spatial datasets and insights that supported critical planning decisions in the region. The project not only tested my technical skills but also strengthened my ability to lead end-to-end geospatial workflows under real-world constraints.

One of the most challenging yet fulfilling projects I’ve worked on is DesignRepo—a curated platform that helps designers discover high-quality tools and resources across the web. The idea came from my own pain point: I was constantly switching between tabs, Notion docs, and bookmarks to track useful design links. There was no single, elegant space that combined all of it in a meaningful, searchable way.

The Challenge:

The project was deceptively complex. I wasn’t just designing a UI—I was trying to solve information overload for designers while also making the platform aesthetic, lightweight, and scalable.

Technical Hurdles:
Creating a CMS structure flexible enough to handle many categories and content types, while ensuring discoverability.

Optimizing the UX for browsing vs. searching, knowing that some users come to explore, and others with a specific intent.

Implementing smart tagging, filtering, and layout responsiveness within Framer, while maintaining performance.

Non-Technical Hurdles:
Curation fatigue — it took hours of manual sorting to separate high-quality tools from fluff.

Overcoming my own creative blocks, especially around launching before I felt it was “perfect.”

Figuring out how to present the platform in a way that didn’t feel like “yet another link dump.”

How I Ultimately Succeeded:

I adopted a lean MVP mindset: launched a minimal version fast, and used feedback to guide the next steps. I built the platform entirely on Framer using CMS collections and custom layouts. I also created a brand voice that felt friendly and clean, making the experience more inviting.

The result? I’ve received organic traction from fellow designers, and it’s helped position me as a builder—not just a designer. It also gave me confidence that I can take an idea from frustration to solution using the tools and mindset I’ve developed.

Kisanserv is an innovative retail company specializing in the sale of fresh fruits and vegetables. Founded on October 15, 2015, the company has established a unique presence in India's grocery retail market by focusing on quality, convenience, and customer experience. Kisanserv operates through an omnichannel approach, blending physical neighbourhood stores with a robust online platform, providing customers with multiple ways to shop.

Direct Farm Sourcing:
Kisanserv sources its produce directly from a network of over 11,000 farmers, ensuring that customers receive fresh, high-quality fruits and vegetables. This direct sourcing method reduces the number of intermediaries, resulting in fresher produce with a longer shelf life.

Omnichannel Retail Experience:
Kisanserv operates convenient neighbourhood stores in urban metro cities like Pune and Mumbai. These stores are designed to provide a premium shopping experience with a focus on hygiene, service, and ambiance.

The company also offers a seamless online shopping experience through the Kisanserv Express app, enabling customers to order fresh produce for quick delivery within 20-30 minutes or opt for in-store pickup.

Affordable Pricing:
By eliminating intermediaries in the supply chain, Kisanserv offers superior quality produce at competitive price.

Focus on Food Safety and Hygiene:
Kisanserv is committed to food safety, selling nearly 100% of its fruits and vegetables in packed form. This approach minimizes direct contact with the produce, ensuring a higher level of hygiene and safety for customers.

Customer-Centric Approach:
Kisanserv's stores are designed to offer a superior shopping experience, with professionally trained staff, a welcoming ambiance, and a focus on customer service. The company also maintains a broad product range, with over 250 items available to meet diverse customer needs.

column47:
One of the most challenging and rewarding projects I led was building a complete performance marketing and content ecosystem for a healthcare brand looking to scale its business and online presence across India. At the time, the client had just one physical center, minimal digital visibility, and no structured funnel for online acquisition.

The Problem:
The brand’s goal was to expand from 1 to 5 healthcare centers within two years and build an online content-led presence to support both acquisition and credibility. The challenge?

Highly competitive category (health & wellness) with strict advertising restrictions.

Low awareness and trust in the brand.

A need for constant educational content, but no in-house creative or strategy team.

Budget constraints in the early stages.

Technical and Non-Technical Hurdles:
Technical:

Building a full-funnel system from scratch—SEO optimization, YouTube growth, Instagram marketing, performance campaigns.

Understanding healthcare regulations for advertising (especially around Google and Meta policies).

Optimizing a high-volume content pipeline for organic and paid growth.

Scaling paid campaigns while maintaining a strict CAC threshold.

Non-Technical:

Training the client’s internal team to adopt digital-first marketing workflows.

Managing stakeholder expectations across marketing, operations, and medical advisors.

Maintaining brand sensitivity and empathy while still being conversion-focused.

Aligning content creators, media buyers, and ops teams across multiple locations.

What We Did:
Developed a YouTube-first content strategy with expert interviews, patient testimonials, and educational series.

Grew their YouTube subscriber base to 1 million+ organically and Instagram to 15k+.

Designed SEO-driven landing pages and optimized the customer journey with CRO best practices.

Ran localized Meta and Google Ads campaigns tailored to specific service lines and geographies.

Created SOPs for content, campaign reviews, lead routing, and performance monitoring.

Consulted on expansion marketing strategy for the new centers, including physical brand visibility and local partnerships.

The Outcome:
Helped the brand grow from 1 to 5 centers in less than 24 months.

Delivered consistent month-on-month lead growth with 250% ROI on the paid media side.

Established the brand as a thought leader in its niche through video content and search rankings.

Built a marketing foundation that remained sustainable even after we transitioned out of day-to-day execution.

This project pushed me to operate at the intersection of content, performance, operations, and long-term brand building—proving that with the right systems, even early-stage brands can scale like digital-first enterprises.

column47:
One of the most challenging projects I’ve led was launching a client-facing app for a high-value but extremely indecisive client. The scope kept shifting, priorities changed mid-sprint, and feedback came late or conflicted with what was said earlier. It wasn’t just a difficult launch, it took a toll on the entire team’s energy and morale, including mine.

The problem: The client involved multiple stakeholders, none of whom had clear decision-making power or vision. They frequently revisited decisions, even after features were designed, developed, and tested. What should’ve been a 2–3 month launch turned into a drawn-out, chaotic cycle of rebuilding and uncertainty.

The hurdles:
>Emotional burnout: The team was tired. Devs were frustrated building features only to later revoke them back and designers were stuck in rework loops. I felt it too. All the planning, and nothing seemed to move forward.

>Lack of clarity: The client’s communication was vague, scattered, and delayed. I ended up spending late nights decoding their messages, piecing together feedback, and constantly updating the team only to change direction again.

>Internal tension: The stress of the product started to affect team dynamics. Energy was low. We were operating, but not thriving.

What we tried:
To bring structure to the chaos, I introduced sign-off checkpoints where no work could proceed unless the client approved the scope in writing. I created a visual roadmap to help them see how much rework their indecision was causing, and to make it visible without blame.

The outcome:
We launched the app. It wasn’t smooth, and it came later than expected, but we did it. But what I took away wasn’t just about shipping a product, it was about what it takes to lead when things don’t go right.

That project taught me how to protect my team’s mental bandwidth, how to push back without burning bridges, and when to step away for the sake of clarity. It was one of the hardest launches I’ve experienced, but easily the one I grew from the most.

column47:
One of my most challenging and rewarding projects was my first product as a startup co-founder in the pet services space. I initially underestimated the domain, only to realize how unstructured the pet ecosystem was—no community hubs, very few data sources, and scattered services. I spent mornings meeting dog walkers, visiting government clinics, pet stores, and suppliers—then used those insights to build out our offering.

We partnered with a SaaS provider to launch the app, manually curated the catalog, and had to resolve pricing transparency with local dealers to ensure fair billing. Marketing was another learning curve—after multiple attempts, I figured out what kind of content actually engaged pet parents. To stand out from local shops, we brought together every pharmacy in the city to offer rare medicines and later integrated Razorpay and Shiprocket to take the platform fully online. Eventually, we became well known in the city’s pet community.

One of the most challenging projects I led involved delivering a complex software solution under tight deadlines and with a high level of ambiguity at the start. The requirements were evolving, and I had to work closely with multiple stakeholders—from product managers and designers to QA and DevOps teams—to clarify objectives and align on a clear, actionable design.

Challenges faced:

Ambiguous requirements requiring continuous refinement and alignment.
Coordinating across diverse teams with different priorities and communication styles.
Tight deadlines demanding fast yet reliable architectural decisions and development.
How I succeeded:

I took initiative to facilitate regular cross-functional meetings to gather feedback and clarify goals.
Developed a flexible but clear design blueprint that balanced immediate needs with scalability.
Prioritized tasks effectively, breaking down the project into manageable milestones to ensure steady progress.
Maintained transparent communication and managed expectations with stakeholders throughout the project lifecycle.
By driving collaboration, maintaining focus on the core goals, and adapting quickly to changes, we were able to deliver the project on time with high quality. This experience strengthened my ability to lead through uncertainty and build consensus in fast-paced environments.

column47:
One of the most technically challenging projects I worked on was designing a logistics pricing constraint system to dynamically calculate delivery charges for buyers in real time — specifically when they load the cart screen.

The complexity came from the diverse and evolving nature of business inputs. The system had to consider a range of constraints, including:

Geographical constraints like the buyer’s location, warehouse proximity, and the ruralness of the area.

Product-level constraints like verticals (e.g. electronics vs grocery), weight, and bundling logic.

Order-level constraints such as whether it’s the buyer’s first order or if the total order value crosses a threshold.

The constraint system had to be highly modular, performant, and scalable — capable of supporting frequent configuration changes from the business team without requiring code deployments. It also had to be consistent and accurate, as pricing directly impacted user experience and margins.

Technical hurdles included:

Designing a fast, extensible evaluation engine that could handle multiple rule types.

Maintaining low latency under high traffic, since the API is hit at cart-load time.

Ensuring data consistency while syncing rules from a configuration service without downtime.

Non-technical challenges involved:

Collaborating with multiple business stakeholders to define and prioritize rules clearly.

Creating a self-serve config interface for non-engineers, while enforcing validations to prevent invalid or conflicting constraints.

Gaining alignment across logistics, product, and pricing teams, who often had differing views on prioritization and defaults.

Ultimately, the system became a critical part of the pricing flow, significantly improving flexibility for the business and keeping user-facing experiences seamless. It has scaled well and has become the foundation for other rule-based pricing logic in adjacent services.

column47:
One of the most challenging projects I regularly face is building investor presentations for our leadership team. We've raised over $95 million to date, but each deck needs to tell the same story in a completely new way — depending on the audience or timing. That repetition, without losing creativity, can be mentally exhausting.

The toughest part is often the ambiguity — the actual pitch or story exists in someone’s head, but getting it onto slides in a way that resonates is the challenge. I’ve spent weeks on versions that looked great to me, only to get feedback like ‘it’s not giving,’ and had to go back to the drawing board.

Over time, I learned to shift my approach. I started sending rough wireframes early — just structure and flow — to get alignment before investing time in design. I asked better questions upfront and built checkpoints to navigate feedback from multiple decision-makers.

It’s still tough — what one stakeholder loves, another might not. But it’s taught me to manage ambiguity, take feedback objectively, and build high-stakes communication under pressure.

One of the most challenging and defining projects I’ve undertaken was building my AI-focused startup from scratch without formal coding expertise, funding, or a strong professional network.

The Problem:
I envisioned creating intelligent, AI-driven tools to solve business inefficiencies, but faced three major obstacles:
	1.	Lack of technical skills — I didn’t know how to code when I started.
	2.	Limited financial resources — I had no external funding or financial backing.
	3.	No access to mentors or a professional network — which made it hard to validate ideas, find collaborators, or attract early users.

How I Overcame It:
	•	I adopted a self-driven learning approach, studying no-code and low-code platforms, open-source projects, and AI tools like ChatGPT to prototype and build functional solutions without deep programming.
	•	I focused on building a strong digital presence—sharing ideas, building in public, and reaching out through social media and communities to attract early users, collaborators, and visibility.
	•	I created MVPs using tools like Bubble, Webflow, and automation platforms like Zapier and Make, and integrated AI services like GPT and Google Cloud APIs to deliver value with minimal engineering effort.
	•	Despite resource constraints, I maintained a relentless focus on problem-solving, learning iteratively, improving based on user feedback, and constantly pushing the boundaries of what I could do without traditional backing.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impressive projects I’ve worked on was building my plus-size fashion brand from scratch—at a time when size inclusivity wasn’t even part of mainstream conversation in India. The idea was bold but came with immense challenges.

The problem: There was a massive gap in the market, but the customer base was underserved, skeptical, and lacked confidence in online shopping for fashion. Manufacturing was a nightmare—factories weren’t used to larger size blocks, and creating flattering fits required deep technical understanding of patterns and body proportions.

Technical hurdles:

Creating sizing systems beyond the standard XS-XL range.

Managing inventory for a wide size range while maintaining profitability.

Educating tailors and production units unfamiliar with plus-size specs.

Non-technical hurdles:

Changing perceptions of beauty and encouraging body confidence.

Building trust with a sensitive customer segment through storytelling and representation.

Running operations as a bootstrapped founder without a background in fashion tech or VC funding.

Despite these challenges, we grew organically, gained loyal customers, and built a brand that made women feel seen and celebrated. Though I eventually exited due to sustainability issues, the journey taught me everything about grit, empathy, and creating businesses rooted in emotional value.

column47:
One of the most challenging and rewarding projects I’ve worked on was leading a commercial to residential conversion in the UK taking it from acquisition and feasibility, planning approval to all the way through to sales while working remotely from India.

The challenge was layered. I was managing stakeholders across time zones, trying to understand and work within the intricacies of UK planning policy, and at the same time ensuring the design made commercial sense for the investors.

On the technical side, the biggest hurdle was understanding what was realistically possible on the site without having a look at the site in person even once. I had to interpret planning guidelines, building nuances and understand local development restrictions and then translate all that into design options that were both practical and profitable. I had prepared detailed feasibility reports, layouts to maximize space and value, and pulled together the full planning application package before the property was purchased by somebody else.

On the non-technical side, communication was a real test. I was collaborating with planning officers, investors and contractors in the UK while sitting halfway across the world. Over time, I have learnt to build robust SOPs, reporting templates, and communication to keep everyone on the same page and build trust.

In the end, the project got planning approval and turned out to be a financial success, we sold the property at a 50% ROI in less than 8 months. But more than that, it was proof that with the right structure and use of tech it’s possible to lead high stakes property development remotely and still deliver real value.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impactful projects I led was during my tenure at Jardine Lloyd Thompson India (JLT India), where I was tasked with transforming and optimizing the pension calculation process.
The Problem: The pension calculation process was complex, highly manual, and heavily reliant on a few key individuals based in London and Mumbai. This created a significant risk due to key-person dependency, and the process itself was time-consuming and prone to delays, affecting overall service delivery and client satisfaction.
Technical and Non-Technical Hurdles: From a technical standpoint, there were no standardized tools or automation in place to handle repetitive tasks. Calculations required manual effort, which led to inefficiencies. On the non-technical side, the challenge was managing change across geographically dispersed teams, overcoming resistance to process transition, and ensuring quality was maintained during knowledge transfer and team restructuring.
The Solution and Execution:
* I designed and implemented a user-developed pension calculation tool, which saved approximately 60 hours of workforce time every month.
* Spearheaded the transition of the entire process from London and Mumbai to Pune, setting up a parallel team to eliminate single points of failure.
* Built a multifunctional team structure, successfully reducing dependency on key personnel.
* Developed a maker-checker review mechanism that significantly improved accuracy and earned recognition from onshore management.
* Achieved 100% backup within the team, which ensured continuity and transformed the operation from person-dependent to process-driven.
Outcome: The result was a drastically more resilient, efficient, and scalable process. It not only enhanced delivery timelines (from the target date to -3) but also contributed directly to client satisfaction and operational stability. This project received appreciation from senior leadership and became a reference model for similar transitions across other functions.

column47:
In one of our enterprise automation projects, we had to automate the validation of
daily sales data between two different sources. The challenge wasn't just data
comparison-it involved conditionally triggering an Automation Anywhere bot from
Automic Workflow, based on the status of several prerequisite jobs. Additionally, the
workflow had to pause if validation wasn't completed, to prevent premature email
dispatches.

I was responsible for designing a solution that could coordinate between Automic and
Automation Anywhere. The system needed to verify the job statuses, decide whether
to pause or proceed, and only then trigger the bot-all in a robust and reliable way
that satisfied both technical and business requirements.

To address this, I first learned how Automic Workflows and their APIs work and how
to interface them using Python. I developed a Python script that checked the status
of prerequisite jobs via Automic API, added breakpoints in the workflow if needed,
and triggered the Automation Anywhere bot via its API once all checks passed.

I collaborated closely with the Automic team and stakeholders through multiple
discussions. Two initial approaches were rejected due to business concerns and
robustness issues. After refining the design, we finalized a solution that was deployed
directly on the Automic server as a decision-making layer.

The final implementation automated what was previously a manual, error-prone
process. It ensured validations were complete before any emails went out and
introduced a new Python-based orchestration model within the team. It not only
saved manual effort but also boosted reliability and trust in the workflow, making it
one of our most impactful and innovative solutions.

column47:
One of the most technically and operationally challenging projects I’ve led is "Cosmic Ray Composition Classification" work using ML on QGSJet simulation data (under GRAPES-3, TIFR Ooty). The aim: distinguish cosmic ray particle types based on shower characteristics by supervised learning ML

Technical hurdles:

Data scarcity and noise in simulated particle events

Preprocessing large-scale cosmic data for model training

Finding a balance between ensemble ML models (like RFC, Bagging) and performance

Non-technical hurdles:

Pitching the idea toa large group of people and convincing and bringing together a diverse team of ML learners who are all working professional adults.

Communicating scientific nuances across these interdisciplinary members

Writing scientific code while ensuring real-time collaboration

Result: Built a modular ML pipeline, benchmarked multiple models, and are now publishing results to present to the TIFR team.

column47:
One of the most challenging projects I’ve led was building Slay Media from scratch — as a solo founder with no prior entrepreneurial experience, during a highly volatile post-COVID market. I had to hire a lean but skilled team, set up workflows for 360° digital execution, and build credibility in a crowded agency space.

The most complex problem came when we scaled from 3 to 10+ clients simultaneously without losing creative quality. We were manually managing briefs, feedback loops, deadlines — which led to burnout and missed moments. The non-technical hurdle was getting the team to shift mindset from “more effort = better output” to “smarter systems = sustainable scale.”

I ultimately built a custom internal ops system that streamlined ideation, client management, and delegation. This not only improved output quality and team bandwidth but also gave me time back as a founder to think bigger — including how to integrate AI more deeply into our DNA.

column47:
The recent one had been to get the sales up in the Nordics for Impossible Cloud. Impossible Cloud is an S3 object storage provider which companies mostly use to store data backups through various backup software like Acronis, Veeam and so on.

After selling to distributors, we had to help distributors resell and penetrate in the market. 

So, our plan was to sell yearly, 3-year and 5-year plan with quantity and duration discounts. I also initiated conversation with quite a few customers having petabytes of data but nothing came closer to closing a deal. Then I shifted more of my time to smaller customers having 50 - 300 TB of data. There I closed deals but could hardly sell long-term high value deals.

I observed this trend happening all across the regions and I suggested our VP sales that we need to rethink our GTM. Since VP had pressure from the top, he was reluctant.

I reached out to the tech team and observed the data ingest patterns of all the customers I've closed so far. Not only that I called each one of them and asked them tailored questions based on their data usage.

As I understood more of them, I saw our GTM was very much flawed.

Most of them said we don't like vendor lock-in - they don't want to buy upfront 1, 3, 5-year plan because their past experience with long-term plans have been poor, the cost savings paying yearly as compared to paying monthly were not justified, and migration was a huge pain for them while getting started. Storage costs come quite at bottom of the list in terms of priority.

Now observing the competition, we couldn't raise our monthly fee. Looking at distributor margins, and we couldn't offer heavier discounts on long term plans. So discounting was ruled out as my option. So, I knew my product would only sell on monthly plan!

Now to drive up demand from existing customers, I focused purely on service. Got every customer on call with our solution architect and actually resolved each and every hurdle in their way. In a single month the utilisation increased by 1.5x. 

Also, I figured out the top clients, offered them yearly plan with a monthly payment schedule. They got interested but couldn't be implemented until I was in the company due to tech limitations.

I scaled my inside sales, took training from the best, became fairly good at cold calling and in general meeting people at in-field sales events as well. This was a pain, and I was only successful enough here. I got 4 new customers in the quarter for around 500TB. I did train other SDRs which actually booked more than 7-10 meetings in a week.

So overall I achieved nice results boosting up sales in the Nordics.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impactful projects I worked on involved extracting structured data from hundreds of dossier PDFs submitted by various pharmaceutical companies. These documents contained critical study-level information, but each company used a different format, layout, and terminology, making it extremely difficult to automate the process.
Each PDF had a unique structure—some were scanned images, others had nested tables or multi-column layouts.
I built a custom parsing pipeline using Python tools like pdfplumber, PyMuPDF, and pytesseract for OCR.
Developed adaptive logic to handle layout variations, including regex-based extraction, keyword mapping, and conditional parsing.
Created a normalization layer to unify terminology and map fields across companies to a consistent schema.
The final product was a consolidated, structured Excel file that enabled cross-study and cross-sponsor analysis. It saved weeks of manual effort, improved data accessibility, and laid the foundation for future automation in regulatory document processing.

column47:
project: building KPI metrics on Power BI dashboard across different domains for a manufacturing client

One of the key challenges in delivering this project was integrating data from various departments within the manufacturing company to accurately calculate and display KPI metrics on the Power BI dashboard. Each domain had its own systems and data structures, making it difficult to create a unified view. In addition to the technical hurdles, we faced resistance from different data owners who were hesitant to share access or make changes to their data sources. Aligning these stakeholders required ongoing discussions to highlight the value of a centralized KPI dashboard for decision-making. By fostering collaboration, establishing clear data governance practices, and iteratively refining the dashboard based on collective input, we were able to successfully bring together the necessary data and deliver a solution that met the client’s goals.

Background: I went to college for electrical engineering, didn't like it and also was terrible at it. Couldnt get a single internship during the 4 years. I really liked UX design. So I went for a 2 year masters in industrial design. 

Then began my professional journey:
1) Consulting internship: In 2018, I couldn't score any UX design internships after year 1 of my masters program. I did get an interview and offer from a company called BCG digital ventures to work as a Strategic Design intern. This company did design thinking and new product innovation consulting for multiple companies. It was a subsidiary of a larger consulting company called BCG (bcg.com).
During this internship I learned a lot of core consulting skills, innovation workshop facilitation and got reps at many rounds of enterprise customer research (via 1:1 customer interviews as well as surveys). I got a return offer from BCG to join after I graduated. In my last year of college I tried to aim for Big Tech UX design jobs but couldn't get any. Got offers from some smaller companies but the offer wasn't as good as the BCG offer so I ended up joining BCG digital ventures.
2) First job as a design strategy consultant: I joined BCG digital ventures in 2019 as a full time Strategic Design Associate (and was there till 2021). My role was essentially to be a specialist in facilitating project teams through the design thinking process to discover new business opportunities for a client. The teams always had a lot of Tech specific talent - designers, engineers, product managers, business folks. My job was to facilitate workshop activities to find new business problems, validate them with customer research. Then facilitate more workshops activities to ideate solutions, collaborate with the Tech team to develop multiple creative concepts, test those with more customer research and finally priorities 1 concept for the team to take to the MVP building stage. I worked on 2 large scale innovation projects. I also worked on smaller scope UX research projects to simply improve the UX of some product or define some new feature for an existing tech company.

3) Then i joined a Real Estate tech startup in 2021. I was recruited by a former BCG manager. My job was very focused on customer research and figuring out the problems that users of the product had with both the platform as well as the service (partially automated) that we provided. I was in charge of reporting on a metric called NPS to my boss and the c-suite of the company.

4) In July 2022 lost my US visa and then had to move back to Mumbai. I struggled with job applications and then in November 2022, got recruited to BCG Digital Ventures again in Singapore.

column44:
Biases and Limitations Pilots Face in Current Aviation Apps

Despite the advancement of technology in aviation, many software applications used by pilots today still suffer from design and functionality biases that limit their effectiveness in real-world flight operations. These include:

⸻

1. Developer-Centric Design Bias

Most aviation apps are built by engineers or tech teams who lack first-hand flying experience. As a result, features are often designed based on assumptions rather than actual cockpit workflow, leading to:
	•	Complex interfaces
	•	Non-intuitive navigation
	•	Redundant or irrelevant information
	•	Poor support for time-critical in-flight decision-making

⸻

2. Airline/Organization Bias

Many apps are tailored to the needs of airline operations control (AOC) or management, not the pilot. For example:
	•	Excessive focus on regulatory documentation
	•	Limited in-flight flexibility or custom pilot inputs
	•	Lack of quick-access tools for real-time decisions like diversions, fuel re-calculations, or alternate planning

⸻

3. One-Size-Fits-All Approach

Many aviation apps are built as universal tools, but fail to consider:
	•	Aircraft type differences (e.g., turboprop vs jet)
	•	Regional operational needs (e.g., VFR-heavy vs IFR-heavy areas)
	•	Pilot experience levels (student vs senior airline captain)

⸻

4. Overload of Data Without Prioritization

Pilots are often flooded with raw data, weather layers, charts, and NOTAMs without intelligent filtering. This creates:
	•	Information fatigue
	•	Slower reaction time in dynamic environments
	•	Increased cognitive load during critical phases like approach or emergency

⸻

5. Lack of Offline Functionality or Poor Integration

In remote or in-flight conditions, many apps become unreliable due to:
	•	Heavy dependence on internet connectivity
	•	No synchronization across devices
	•	Poor integration with aircraft systems or EFBs

⸻

Conclusion

The most intersting  project I worked on was cross selling credit cards with Home loans. When I joined the bank a directive came from the MD that credit card sales need to go up. All the business groups were given targets to sell Credit Cards along with their own products. While credit cards complement a lot of products in the industry,Home loans was not one of them. I did a root cause analysis on why the adoption for our sales team was low. Keep in mind I was new and had no idea about the stake holders. I had to multiple sales team interview and also visited a few of our customers. I realised that sales were not able to explain the value proposition of the cards and a lot of the customers already had the basic card. The solution, I did a 2 Day training with internal certification making the sales team aware of the credit card value propositions and also with multiple discussion  with the credit card product team made it clear that all the home loan customers will be eligible for the premium and some for the ultra premium credit card which was an invite only card. Also we made it clear to the sales team that the card propositions to be told to the customers only when their home loan credit is cleared. 

This remains one of the most special projects because this was being observed by the top management and with these solutions the adoption actually improved from 35% to 80%.

column47:
One of the most challenging and impactful projects I worked on was Flow, an open-source design system built for enterprise-grade products. (https://flow.ollion.com/)

The problem was that multiple teams across products were designing in silos, leading to inconsistent UI, duplicated efforts, and poor accessibility. We needed a unified design language that could scale across use cases, tech stacks, and team maturity levels.

On the technical side, we had to ensure token-based theming, flexible component architecture, and Figma-to-code alignment. I collaborated closely with developers to create usage guidelines that worked across platforms.

On the non-technical side, getting buy-in was the bigger challenge. Each product team had its own priorities. So I ran audits, facilitated workshops, and presented tangible benefits. Faster velocity, fewer bugs, better UX consistency.

Ultimately, Flow became the foundation for multiple product lines. It reduced design-debt, aligned engineering efforts, and improved time-to-market. It was a rare project where strategy, systems thinking, and deep collaboration all came together.

For me, getting into a flow state happens when I'm deeply immersed in a project that challenges me intellectually and creatively. It’s when I’m working on something I’m passionate about, like designing a 3D model, solving complex problems in AI, or fine-tuning a UI. The process needs to be engaging enough to capture my full attention and allow me to lose track of time. I get into flow when the task is at the right level of difficulty—demanding but not overwhelming.

When I’m in the zone, I’m fully focused on the task at hand, whether it's creating something new or optimizing a system. The ability to experiment, learn, and iterate freely without distractions also contributes to this state. Music that complements the task or silence often enhances my concentration. I tend to feel most energized and productive when I’m experimenting with new techniques in 3D art or AI and seeing the results take shape in real-time.

In a broader sense, flow happens when I feel aligned with the task's purpose and my long-term goals, which could be learning a new programming language or finishing a project that moves me closer to my career aspirations.

column47:
Handpicked by leadership to take charge of a cross-functional project with teams across engineering, services, implementation, success, and sales, I noticed that not everyone was engaging during team meetings. Though I did not pay much attention to it earlier, when we hit a roadblock, leading to a rework of 2 weeks and putting project deadlines at risk, I knew I needed to introspect.
While I had successfully delivered multiple projects previously, this was my first time leading such a large-scale project. Further, out of the 17 team members, there were many highly-experienced seniors, whom I would usually turn to for guidance. I realized that deferring team decisions to these few seniors had made me overlook inputs from others, resulting in some members feeling unheard and underappreciated.
Taking guidance from my manager, I realized I had to change my way of working. To ensure everyone was involved in team decisions, I conducted an all-hands meeting to take inputs from all the teams to understand the issues, rectify project direction. As such large-scale projects required flexibility and adaptability as per the situation, I also set up regular check-ins to assess and recalibrate our strategy if required. This empowered the team to take ownership of the tasks and bring in innovative ideas eventually helping us close the project a week before the deadline despite initial delays.
While this experience made me aware of my shortcomings, it also helped me realize the true essence of being a democratic leader who takes input from everyone, empowers the team to share their diverse perspectives, and makes them feel valued.

column33:
Keyur Modi is a name that has become synonymous with digital innovation, content entrepreneurship, and grassroots storytelling in Gujarat. A visionary at heart and a leader by execution, Keyur is the Founder of Surties, a pioneering regional digital media brand; a strategic Investor in emerging content and tech ventures; and a compelling Podcaster who amplifies voices from every walk of life. With over a decade of experience at the intersection of media, marketing, and storytelling, his journey reflects relentless passion, creative agility, and a commitment to community-driven impact.

🔹 The Founder: Building Surties from Scratch
Keyur’s entrepreneurial journey began in 2013, when the idea of starting a digital media platform focused on Surat seemed unconventional. While mainstream media focused on metros and national headlines, Keyur saw a massive opportunity in hyperlocal storytelling. On 26th December 2013, he launched Surties, initially on Facebook, to document the stories of Surat—its people, places, struggles, celebrations, and culture.

From posting photographs and short stories, Surties gradually expanded into a multi-platform digital brand with a strong presence on Instagram, Facebook, YouTube, and a dedicated website. In 2019, post-COVID, Keyur transformed Surties into a full-scale digital news channel, offering video explainers, podcasts, civic reports, and interviews. Today, with 700,000+ followers and widespread brand trust, Surties has become the voice of Surat’s digital generation.

Keyur’s leadership style blends editorial integrity with platform intelligence—understanding what the audience needs, how they consume, and how to stay relevant in the ever-changing content landscape.

🔹 The Investor: Nurturing Local Talent & Innovation
Beyond Surties, Keyur has positioned himself as a micro-investor and mentor in the domains of content creation, digital marketing, and technology-led media ventures. He believes that Surat and Gujarat have untapped creative potential that needs nurturing—through capital, guidance, and exposure.

He has played a key role in mentoring creators, podcast startups, video production houses, and digital brands, helping them scale through strategy, storytelling, and brand building. His goal is to build a self-sustaining ecosystem of Gujarati digitalpreneurs who don’t need to migrate to metros to grow.

🔹 The Podcaster: Stories That Matter
As the host of The Surties Podcast, Keyur has created a platform that goes beyond the news—it gets personal. His podcast features entrepreneurs, doctors, artists, police officials, athletes, innovators, and even unsung heroes from Surat and beyond. These conversations focus on life experiences, leadership, challenges, and vision, offering both inspiration and insight to a wide listener base.

With over 50+ podcast episodes, Keyur’s conversational style is empathetic, real, and thought-provoking. He’s not just interviewing—he’s curating a cultural archive of stories that matter.

🔹 Thought Leadership and Community Impact
Keyur has also emerged as a thought leader and speaker on topics such as:

Digital Journalism & Media Ethics

Instagram Content Strategy

AI in Digital Marketing

Regional Content Revolution

Entrepreneurship from Tier-2 Cities

He frequently delivers guest lectures at universities, mentors media students, and participates in startup incubators as a domain expert.

Looking Ahead
Keyur Modi envisions building Surties into a Pan-India hyperlocal storytelling platform, launching regional verticals and empowering citizen journalism. Whether it’s crafting viral content, discovering voices from the ground, or investing in tomorrow’s creators, Keyur is at the forefront of India’s digital media evolution—leading not from the spotlight, but from the grassroots.

column47: One of the most challenging and impactful projects I led was the re-encryption of massive volumes of enterprise PII data using HashiCorp Vault as part of a compliance and security overhaul. This required me to learn and apply complex regex patterns to support format-preserving encryption algorithms that maintained data schema integrity across millions of records. A key technical hurdle was ensuring that certain characters—such as backslashes—that could be misinterpreted during decryption were excluded or handled correctly, which meant we had to carefully sanitize and re-encrypt the entire dataset to prevent corruption. Additionally, we orchestrated this huge operation with minimal downtime while retrofitting legacy systems to integrate with Vault’s APIs. Non-technical challenges included extensive coordination among security, DevOps, and application teams, alongside strict compliance audit requirements. Through phased rollout, automation leveraging HashiCorp Vault’s Transit and Transform engines, and close collaboration, we successfully re-encrypted all PII data, ensuring regulatory compliance and a scalable security framework for the future.
